speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In this interview, Dora Flener discusses an account of homesteading near Moscow in the 1870s that she heard from her father -in -law Dave Flener, proof that author William Drannon and Wild Davey were separate individuals working out at various jobs before marriage, life on threshing operations, home remedies for illnesses, and the advent of cars.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The shelter that he built when he first came here. I think he must have brought some stuff with him like billboards and things like that when he came and brought his wagon and team up from Walla Walla country. He built his first house and put a canvas over the top of his tarp that he used for and just built a kind of a two board high for it. It was open winter that winter, so it wasn't cold or anything. There was much snow on these horses. He stayed going out and no one had to stay by. One thing they had a lot of trouble with in the early days was that the people were driving horses through the country to Montana and they'd pick up everything that was loose. Sometimes the other horses that was running on the range, they let them out. They would pick them up themselves, colts and stuff.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: They'd come and see horses coming by. They'd come to the band and horses and they'd just include them in the buns. They wouldn't try to separate them out. They'd lost several that way. Two year olds or something like that. Sometimes they'd round them up on purpose. They were at the name of doing that anyway. Montana needed the horses for some reason. I don't know what. That was after he came there, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever mention the vigilante days?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, he did too. Of course, he wasn't in, but he heard about them. He said he was a woman that didn't come back and didn't come back and she spoke. That's what the man he's called him. He hung him up, strung him up. It was going on in other parts of Idaho. He'd come around here, you know, cattle, horse, horse, horse, horse leaving, I guess, horse and cattle leaving at that time. He didn't have any good person to himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he heard this around where he lived?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Oh, yes. Another thing I didn't include in my part of it was the first lights they had. Lared oil, they'd use it in a can and on crimp they'd use it in a can. They'd put a wick of a cloth in there and lighten it that.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That's a pretty basic way to get your light, I guess. That's what I'd say. It wouldn't be much light. Like a smoky kind of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the social life of those real early days?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, they had dances. Mostly dances and parties.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: By all of us, the first towns close by, you know, by all of the blues. And those people congregated in theirs and theirs and died in saloons too in those days high old time, I guess.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But they used to go horseback riding too early. They'd need to go, you know, in the meantime, every time of the year I guess horseback was going away. Single man would go. They would do a celebration. I took a wagon there and the whole family went down to Mardin. I was going to have to sit just for the board because of the wagon box, you know, five to five feet across the country I guess. I don't know about going to call fax this board July celebration one time that way. They had somebody stay home and look after their stock on it. They couldn't get back there and get back the same day. They were overnight probably. Celebrate. They'd be trying in those days to use flags and money and all. Things trimmed up great. I remember that in my day, horses and flags and their rivals, you know, and what he said and flags and all. The speaker's stand is all draped in the running red flag blue bunny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you know the roads really ditched about weren't any then at all?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, they weren't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They really was cross country?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: You'd have to go. No, they could. Of course they had their wides on the hills. They had some way of going, you know, some trail of some kind. It'd been a funny thing to be very direct. Directed they could of course, but then some people you couldn't go to. They'd go straight through because of the oak. Then after that, if they didn't get roadless and dusty, then they couldn't hardly stand it, you know. Some of the vehicles came to the country anyway. Gave them such a dust, they couldn't see where they were going. Of course that was later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the night that you talked about that he and his friend thought that they were lost when they were coming down from a dance?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah, I think they would have stepped over that night a long ways off and they were coming back with my eyes. It was kind of foggy and snow on the ground. I couldn't tell their eyes or anything where they were. No fences, no buildings they could see to make any marks. They recognized it. But they, it's high heels thought that they were sure taking the wrong direction all the time. But Davey knew what to do. He gave his horse a free rein, you know, and let it go home by itself. He kept telling these thoughts that they'd tell the high heels that they'd find some kind of a shack or something. They could stay over the rest of the night, but got to get their bearings, you know, later. He kept going, kept going, kept going. Pish and they came to a barn building and he turned his horse, Lucille, right in the barn. He was recognized as their own place. The horse would take them back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever talk to you about any of the old characters that he knew from those days? Any of the people in the country that really stood out as being characters, being different?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I don't think he did.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It was a high, high heel. It was the only one that was with him. And he was near a corpse. Of course it was. Mr. Bold, he was a star keeper at Post Nastia. I don't know if he was quite a character, but people always liked him real well because he, on the side of the people that didn't have much to eat, he'd take wood and, put in place of, trained wood for, trained provisions for wood rather. And that way they got along pretty well, you know. And then his 200th Corps, they sent me to the backyard one time and trained for people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where Dave Fleeter did his training, Viola?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: He did it for a while I guess. A Moscow guy. My own was like three miles from there. And Moscow got farther. About eight miles from there. Seven or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you believe anything else? What about the first plowing that he did? Did he ever talk about it?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yes, he did the first plowing that first year he was there. Because it was such an open winter and he'd do horses and a breaking plow. He brought a breaking plow with him. He would attend some of those beautiful things like that. He didn't even need, you know. He got his lumber to build his next house next year. He did white board and batten, they called it. He built that the next year. He had a better house. He had the lumber I think from Viola or some sawmill around. They were getting up sawmills by that time. And those first boards were immense wide things. You know, about 18 inches wide. Wide board, there's few of them around the country now in old houses and things.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Not being timber, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever tell you how come he turned down Llewelyn's offer to start up in a town there?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, in the first place he didn't want to have any town property. Llewelyn asked him twice to trade with him. He said, let me just build a shack there in town. But he didn't care anything about town. All he could do was keep me up in Homestead, you know. And then he asked him again, trade his data log for the team. Well, he needed the team so he couldn't do that. He wasn't interested.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he talk about seeing how the country got filled up with people?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, yeah, he said there was one party that came across the saddle there. He came to take up the land right across from, I was a roadie boat, and we rode him down on the west side of him. But the woman was sure that there'd never be any. He'd be an isolated country, always like it was then. She didn't like it, so they never even packed, unpacked their stuff. But that was the way. It wasn't long before Mr. Collins came in with his family, so they settled. And so it didn't take long for the country to settle up that time. Railroad commands would come in about 80, 80 or two percent. The English command has to be quite normal, and after that, it took so long to get that grain to wallow on. It took two days, I think, to get all that grain to wallow on their first, their first market, you know, where they get it. And take the grain down there, and then they'd get some provisions for the last of a year, all winter or something like that. I guess I told you about the woman that wanted to buy a pack of needles. She'd find them on the wallow wallow, and I was the one who'd think she'd find the needles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She said, why, how come so much?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And I said, it's a big price. It's a high cost of freight, he told her. Freaked out needles, she didn't think that very much. I guess some of the stuff was afraid to wallow on the river and then the river. They got out the part and not that way, but they were just afraid to fall off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he talk about going to Wallow Wallow himself?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I don't think he did. He tried freighting one time, but he went up, or he took up the claim, I think. He didn't like it freighting because he couldn't use his own team, and I guess he wasn't kind of out of freighting anyway. He didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what was it that he told you about the early harvests that he worked in?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: He worked down at two ships, and go down there in the summertime, and took the money. He gave me the inflation on the part of the year he worked there in the summertime, and harvest, heading outfits, thrashing outfits.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And I guess after they got to finding the heat of this, he cut the bottom of the bed, and the ice was shipped. He defeated it. He had a bundle cutter on each side of him. He'd take the bundles and put them into the machine, you know, loose crates. And of course, it worked pretty fast, and he'd reach out to the bundles, and the fellow was cutting the string at the same time. He might get jabbed, you know. It was pretty risky to do it, really.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Talking about that, I guess, down there, too, and he admitted about the bundle, the thrashing bundle grain. But his job, he was feeding the feeder, and the thrashing machine. He couldn't feed too fast, and too slowly, wanted to keep it up. It's steady, bunching in there all the time, you know. Somebody's an expert to know just how much to put in, how much not to put in.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I wanted to ask you just a couple of things about his earlier days. One thing you mentioned in his writing is that when he saw the Bronc bust, and that the guys who were doing that work were really getting hurt. They'd get the wild horses off of the Bronc, you know, and bring them to ride, and they'd get out of them and just ride them. Not like they do on the rodeos now. They would ride them for eight minutes or three minutes or something like that, and they stayed with them, and they broke them in. He never tried it because he wasn't into the boat. He'd bleed from the ears and the horse, and he'd sit there, jarring, you know. They'd stay with him, though.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That was in his earlier days, in California, I imagine.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And then this long trek that he took into Oregon up over to Siskiyou Summit. Yeah. His partner didn't think they were going to make it. Yeah. He was a kid. He was 16, I think, at the time.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I think that they went from in the stage line, and then they had this too deep snow, and they couldn't stay and run over the bad road and the high parts of the mountain, and they caught as much snow. So they took that. I don't think it took them very long to walk it. Probably just day in the night or something like that.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I think most of the on top of the snow, the snow's crust is hard. They couldn't do it hard to go, but he had to know their way, you know. They get there until he was lost on the way. He hadn't even crossed it before, so he knew about where. And he had to hatch it and axe it. He chopped the places in the trees, and he went along just for fun, I guess. He'd come through there, and see where the high -high -leaved snow was. The way up in the trees, you know, he'd blaze it.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And his friend of his really thought that they weren't going to make it. And they long walked through the kid, you know. He couldn't make it at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then it seems like he really had a lot to do with the Indians, the River Indians, right?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah, he had. In California. He heard cattle there a lot of the time, I think, and they were there real time, too. Just kind of camped out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do my cowboy do sometimes?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: One of those pavilions, probably they just stay in camp there, and there's badlands. Well, they're not badlands, exactly. They have abeds with their spatchets and their weather grass. That's where the cattle grazed.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The Indian country, there it was.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And when he was in his north in California, his own claim, he took a claim there first. He built a shack there, and the Indians used to come and open his door, and didn't knock the door, and walked in. He was hungry, and they kept me. He couldn't feed all of them, so they had to put stuff to that. The dogs would go, so they wouldn't let the Indian come close to the house after a while. He said it's pitiful, but they didn't have any clothes on, not me, anything. He'd see shawl over there. Back, you know, with the people. The United States didn't provide him anything neat or anything. They couldn't leave a reservation to hunt fish like they should. They'd been able to, you know.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And on the state they'd go in, native state, they would go in big campers, you know, and get the reservations for the winter here and there. They'd leave their own part of the country, but on their own invasion, they'd dash the feet. For the reason it made the trouble, I guess someone was bound to go off. I mean, that's the trouble, that they, they aren't trying to make them stay out there. They were so hungry that if they found anything dead, if a cow died or anything, they'd eat it anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long had it been dead?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Because they were so hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were a couple of the funny, amusing incidents that he told you that took place between him and the Indians?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, one of them I remember now was one about the Indian that was cold weather, and he didn't have much on him. Bear, that is your son, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I wanted to find him, give him the spat in the bottom and say, aren't you cold there?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And he said, no fair.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, I say like, like baseball and cold.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: He used to. His partner had two barrel world on, so he gave him a pair.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: He didn't think that they needed to hang those ones that they hung on to paraphrase Charlie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Captain Jack?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That Captain Jack was. He didn't think they were anything that bad. Or he exposed a shot of a soldier or an officer's army or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he say about it? He wasn't down there at the time, was he?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, but he heard about it. He was right there at the time. One of them probably at the time. He probably was in the reservation at that time or that part of the country, but he wasn't there to see the actual trouble. I remember they chased the Indians pretty hard. Yeah, they did.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: You mentioned in here too that when he was younger and his brother had tuberculosis, and they had a pretty unusual kind of cure. Yeah, I had a trumpet. A lot of people on that and they'd strike their lungs.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It made me think they probably didn't know anything what to do. Oh, no, they didn't. I didn't think they could do it hardly. Try anything, you know.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: In this diphtheria epidemic, Dave's father had his own home cure for it. Yeah, Bernal and Breonbark.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I wonder where you ever picked that up from. I read something the other day about early remedies and something. I guess maybe that was in last Whitman County. Corley. Bunch of grass. Historian.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: LMT was good for diphtheria. Didn't say slippery elm, they didn't say anything about Bernal and me either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about wild A .V.?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, quite a lot of supposition about whether he was the same as Will Dannon or not. Will Dannon wrote the book, or he didn't write it, but he had told the story of 30 years in the plains and the mountains, maybe you've heard of it. And the wild A .V. got many people thinking the same man, you know. We knew it was different because the cleaners knew both of them. Because one of the cleaners helped Bernal write it up. He was a newspaper man anyway. So, well, Bernal told him the story and he wrote it, see. And, well, wild A .V. was the father's up in the mountains. He wore a long hair and was about the same size as Bernal. I guess that's the reason he got confused.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But wild A .V. lived in the mountains and he had dogs and he had hunter, he had coyotes and cougars out of his business. It's many dogs that claim he used to eat some of them too.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The story tells about anything at the time that the kids had been told about the time that they, she met wild A .V. See, they lived up in the mountains here, Oscar Mountains, out of the shore there somewhere.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And the women and folks, they used to be afraid of him and they'd lunch up at night and the men were out harvesting and afraid of him. And they would ride and take their kids out horseback and go and stay with each other, you know, stay with the neighbors. But one day Mrs. Kissing herself, wild A .V. would be coming around up toward her house and she was finishing washing the dishes. She met him out at the corner of the house, there was a dishwater over him and he came around to go in the house.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That was a big snip, never bothered anymore I guess. He wasn't going to bother anyway, probably coming. But he used to come, you know, the cleaners would tell him about how he came and one time to look for his horse, his horse and got away and he went to the country trying to find him. So then they knew they didn't know, got to clean with him. And they knew, well, there were two different people and I've run across there with people and they knew they were different here but not very many of the people admitted.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Do you know, I heard that Wild Davey used to tell some pretty wild stories and he used to say he'd done a lot of kinds of stuff that had something to do with why people thought he was drowning. Because he used to, well I heard him call the windjammer. I don't know about that but he was, I know I wouldn't be surprised if I did. There's Mr. Gamble and all that stuff.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Lola, Lola Clyde's brother, he said he used to work in the harvest with him while they used to. He didn't have much sense, he couldn't write a book, he said anything. And his name was O .C. Covington, he said. The right name was.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: On my side and when I tried to prove these things, I can't remember, I'm trying to find his, somebody said he was buried in Troy Cemetery and I was down in Troy one day and I asked the postmasters if she knew where the cemetery was. She said which one, she did a seven, I'm around here so I wasn't about to let him all up to find out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think this guy really did bother the ladies?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No I don't think so. When he looked at me with that long hair and he looked like he was wild, you know, and he named some of the dogs and he lived by himself. I don't know if you can tell him that. But he was, especially if anybody didn't look right I suppose. New country, you know, that kind of. Pray that most anything doesn't look this right I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any other characters like him that you, not like him, but any other characters like you heard about in the country?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I can't recall it right now anyway.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: We knew Matt used to get drunk a lot and he used to stop the schoolhouse and waving hard to us, the teacher, and if we were out playing in the yard, you know.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And one time he came by my home, her mother was out hanging out closing and he stopped yelling at her. And the pain that he was doing, I was scared and deaf. About seven eight years old then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But since we moved, where's Whitman County?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: He moved over there too and he used to come home drunk at night and one night he and his wife sticked all night in the straw stack because he couldn't get home any farther. Another time he went off the road and took over the buggy and he didn't hurt anybody, he lost his hat. I've seen him wear a person anything else because he lost his hat.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: There was people coming and going at night and more or less they noticed to all the authorities got onto it and found they had a cell in their house, a trap door on their pantry, went down.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That's the nearest we ever came to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it cut down the drinking a lot to have prohibition?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I don't know what they said and I don't know what I couldn't tell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about women's, the women's right to vote?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I don't remember anything about that. I don't think it affected us much. At least I don't remember if folks ever say anything about it. Because they weren't people to say much about anything anyway really. And didn't discuss anything. One of us kids seemed like, wish they'd done more of it. I think I'd have been a little bit better informed of the early age than I was. I never spoke anything bad about anybody or any wire I'm here for. I know that we were going to school and I guess about the time that Teddy Roosevelt was coming in to come up for election.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Part of the kids were from my parents and part of Democrats. We heard good, we called them a damn rat. Not to their patient.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: We in Monson girls were Democrats. They were Republican. Their father was in the legislature in Idaho at that time. British and all Republicans.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That's as much as I remember about being in a long politics bubble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the revivals that came to Moscow? Did you ever have anything to do with them?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Only one of them. I think the Lowry meetings, that was the only one I remember anything about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What meetings?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Lowry. I love W .E .R .Y. I was a preacher and Moody was the fire leader. I like to sing so I don't want to go in the fire. I don't think anybody wanted to sing. I can't play them sing. The funny part of it was he had a chair on the platform. I can't remember anyone one chair but had several spindles in the back. You know how those are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what the spindles are don't you?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Explain it to me. The things that go up and down the back and those round things. You wanted to get the attention of the audience or the congregation. He could rip that baton across that and make such a funny noise. Everybody would startle and they'd start talking and pay attention. But that was in a tent. I guess that was in a tent. So I knew about it I remember. Took about a fourth of a block there between Jefferson and 3rd Street. Then I saw this on the floor. I don't know what the hell it was. It was in Baxham anyway. But it was kind of a crude outfit. We had lots of fun in it. That was an awful lot of snow too. People didn't even plow out of the snow. People didn't shove off their walks any year. Tramped the snow down and all over the top of the drift. Packed the snow and kept on the trail. That's all I did. Farmers liked to have snow in town because they had to use sleds. When they come up here downtown they couldn't run. They didn't have any snow plows anyway to clear it off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what winter this was?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: 1912. February 1912.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you perform for the adults? The choir?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, when they gave the order a call we were supposed to leave the platform and go down and mingle with the people. As some of them were questioning if they wouldn't want to be and all that. I wasn't very good at it. I just kind of bluffed it. I didn't know that was very good of words anyway. I was arguing a point. That probably went well too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember? Was it a pretty quiet or noisy kind of a meeting? Did people get pretty excited?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, it wasn't that kind.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I remember it was quite a pain.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It was quite successful as well. I was used to a really loud breach of enmity at this region. I was really stumped around or faced something down the platform or down the pulpit. The poundiest pulpit. It's podium or whatever you call it. So I don't know whether I was used to being a pretty loud preacher anyway. It was ordinary to me.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: You must have been there about two weeks I think. It was my second year of high school I think that year.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I was made up of young people, mostly high school kids I think.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, I wanted to ask you about the early thrashing operations. What are the earliest ones that you know about or how were they when you... Well, I saw a few that were very feeble.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The engine wasn't powerful enough to propel itself. They charged it onto it to pull it out and put it in one place or another, you know.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I heard about somebody telling about a story. I guess it's never been a story about that day. We were talking about early days. The horses were pulling this machine to one side or another and it dropped into a ditch at the front end. We didn't drop a ditch and horses couldn't pull it out. We were talking about that while they were eating dinner one day. When Cook's kind of worried about it, she said, well, they knocked the water clouds at all and the tank was in front of the engine and ran out of the smoke stack and asked them to come pretty close to the ground. I thought that made all the people laugh because the water cloud had saved the toilet in those days, you know.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But it wasn't long before they got some of the engine to pull a separator in.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I didn't know why that was. I don't know, just very few of them that couldn't propel themselves around.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That bigger and bigger and bigger, you know, they couldn't kind of make all the men into homes. And so they had to, somebody called the bride and the real little cook house. That went with the machine always, you know. I guess I'm sorry, I was already two men on the crew there at that time. Outfits.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The money then because real cash, it didn't get any other way ever easy.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Especially if a man had a team he could take out and a team of wagons that paid for pretty good money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the cooking situation like in the cook house?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, it was a pretty awful line thing. They had to get up early in the morning to get the pen. It had to get in long days because so many farmers wanted their work done, you know, before rain came and for the right season. So they got up about 3 .30 in the morning and went to the cook house and put the kerosene lamps to start the fire and breakfast.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The men slept in the straw stack. And then here, I guess, the firemen, I guess, he was the one that woke him up in the morning. He was the one that had to get up the steam, you know, and get firing up. And he had to go right after breakfast. And men had to get up and feed and harvest their horses to work to be done before breakfast. They had to get up early too.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Then cooks. She said, I'm a plant, she tells them about the experience you were. She worked seven years, essentially seven years, the same man. She was saying that they...

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, they had to be real like a now or so with their building. They had to get them on, get their meals on in time. She said they had to fry a whole milk pan full of steaks for breakfast. They had everything there, warm biscuits, you know, bean pie biscuits and everything. They'd go along with eggs and bacon and everything. When they moved they couldn't have... They'd like to pick up the move many times, whether it was the matter whether they were baking or what they were doing. She said they never had lost any cakes or anything like that from falling or anything. They had to hold out the door shut a lot of the time. They couldn't open the door. They had to cook the house. It would be like a mid -lip summer. The first meal. They had to decide if they were going to raise the layer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that a size raised?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, they kind of looked like an awning. They had that water barrel outside where they sent the outside with a platform up. It was part of the cacao stew. The fucking kept that full of water for the cooks. They kept the wood split formed in a handy place. When Town got their moorations formed all... They got their milk from the man from the country. They had coyote night and morning he'd go and get milk for them. The refrigeration they had was just a tub of water that sent the milk in.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: How they got along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she talk about how hard the work was to her?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: She said it was late. Late when they got to work that night, they probably practically met themselves getting up in the morning and down at the market. They didn't come eating. They left and they couldn't crash anymore. They had to do all that work up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the work and other crews out from pictures I've seen?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It looks like it was awful dusty. Sure it was. It was terrible. Well, no. The first machine didn't have any blower either. They had to keep mowing that straw back from the... Draper. They ran out of the Draper and dropped it down. They had to keep mowing that back and that was terrible hard. Nasty work, that chaff. They used to hang a chip over their face and keep reading it so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they had a hard time holding the crews?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, no, no. They loved it. They would just... Well, the men liked to get together for one thing. They had a big time, you know. And they all tried it out with each other with them working in. Of course, they didn't want it better than to do that. It was a very clean way to do anything. Sleeping with the straw stack wasn't very good either. I don't think they'd be pretty chaffy and dirty. They'd be full of chaff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they take much time to socialize when they weren't working? Did they sit and have storytelling sessions?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, they didn't have time. They didn't have time at all. Sometimes they didn't have little time to go and suck eggs. I have a feeling they told about how they used to... One of the young people used to rob that chicken house. Some farmers, they knew we were out in the living care. They couldn't suck the eggs. They'd try to get one of them and they'd suck an egg. He'd just come right back up again. He couldn't stand it. Some would like it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you put a hole in the egg in the second straw?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah. I wouldn't like that at all either.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That's an old saying that I hear sometimes. You sure broke him from sucking eggs. Yeah. They don't want him to suck eggs. They didn't make that much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For most of the guys in the crews, mostly the local boys?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah. They're family men. They need their wives at home. They want to make money. That's about the only money they made during the year. It was real cash. My sister, she used to run without to work. She would always get up and do something important, like a paper and some big job. She couldn't do very well. She had meals to get on. She'd change furniture around. One time she'd change her bedroom and her living room around. She'd change the living room and living room to the bedroom. She came back from work on Saturday night and couldn't find a bed.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But they always worked the fall weekend when they came home. Nobody worked on Sunday in those days. When they started opening or anything, they couldn't buy anything or anything like that. The Blue Law, one time there was a law to have everything open. They'd come home and get a change of clothes and take a bath. I suppose they were jumping a creek or something. They didn't have any bathing facilities at all. They'd shower a bath. That sounds so interesting. It sounds like it was really a big, almost like celebration in a way. It was, yes. Men looking forward to it every year, you know. They'd get together for men. They had a high old time, I guess. They'd put more or less pranks on each other while they worked, but then they didn't stop work to do it.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: In the combine days, I know they used to put a prank on each other, and they'd fill each other's pockets with grease, and they'd grease them. They'd combine and stuff like that.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I didn't have much time. They were older men anyway, usually.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would the Threshen operation be owned by one guy, and everybody come work for them?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yes, that's you, your partner sometimes. You're just one man on the side machine, and he'd get around everyone that he could, you know. He said, well, I'm the country, probably. But they liked to have the best operating machine they wanted. They'd break down some of my sons and his farmers, and they'd speak for him ahead of time, you see. Supposed to come after so -and -so got hit down with it, and they'd have theirs. Well, sometimes he had a breakdown, he couldn't get there, and it looked rainy. The farmer might get somebody else that was handy. That'd make the Threshenman machine man mad. You know, what's going to do is cheat him all that much money. He works the other way, too, that sometimes somebody begged him and paid him a little bit of bonus or something. That one fella wanted to take somebody else's closer. So he worked both ways, and so some hard feelings got started that way. So it was good when they got a combine, so that wasn't going to happen anymore. The Threshenman had his own. It was a partnership, a combine business, doing something that wasn't too good either, because one farmer wanted to do all the adjusting and all the say -so about it, and he was pretty meek when he got left out, and if he wasn't meek, they had harsh words with each other.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Now that he got their own combine, it would cost him a lot of money, but then he went dead up to their ears, and they had more fun with it, you know. The other day they wanted to, as long as they wanted to, and nobody bothered him.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The old Threshenmachines, they were pretty cantankers. They were pretty tickly sometimes, keepin' running. Well, yes, and just like any machinery, they didn't have it up good. I'm a kind of rattle -trap kind of anyway to start in with, you know, older. So I didn't fix my haywire, and it didn't last for me long.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Then just like they did after their combine, now they have to get them in the perfect shape before the students start, and I'm gonna have trouble during the season.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were their jobs that were the top jobs on the operation?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yes, the engineer and the separator man. Because the separator man was the owner, because the separator was the biggest responsibility, to keep it going, knowing that it was about to break down, or it was plugged up sometimes. Watch it pretty carefully. And the firemen he had, and all his business, had to keep up the steam all the time. They weren't strong, and he had to keep stuff in there pretty fast. They fell a little there above everybody else's, in the environment. So it's actually until he was important.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Cook.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But... The two women were cooking together in the honey oven, they were the best ones that they could heal by. There weren't many people that wanted a job cooking, because it was hard work, and it was... You know, girls 18 or so, you know. Single girls are young married women that didn't have the children or responsibilities at home. I'm a plaid shoe, just a young married woman at that time. My husband worked on a machine too. It was Nora Hill, it was partner with her. She was a married girl, 18 I think at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was their pay pretty good, compared to the men working on the harvest?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I don't really know what the pay was. Man, it was pretty good for Cook.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I asked her if she was a head cook, and she said no, they both shared a lot. She said they wanted... She was the one that planted all the meals and helped out, you know. She said, they just worked together. So they got the most of the same pay. I think the three -dollar today was a pretty good pay at that time.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I know men and carpenters, they only got three dollars a day. My dad had somebody build a chicken house, and he would pay three dollars a day for carpenter work.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The man, he had horses and had a team and wagon, and he'd get more for them. I don't know how much more of it. If he had a team and wagon, then he'd haul the grain. He'd haul the bundle, then he'd bundle for her. If he don't pick up the shots, you know. I think quite a number of them, six or eight or ten, I guess, bundle wagons. Racks belong to a... she's an outfit. Running gears, the wagon belongs to men. Team, you know, you bring a wagon. Yep.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And he'd go all over there, pick up the shots. One man was on the ground, and he'd bundle a wagon. The man that was on the ground, he'd take a bundle and throw it up to the fella on the wagon, and the fella on the wagon catching on his fork, and put it in place. So the hand was up and the fella took it down, so that they wouldn't break the hands off. So he loaded the wagon.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: When you remember the early days, do you remember that was after they didn't...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they still using headers in those days?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: No, they didn't use head at all. They'd go down the river, from a two -seat country down there. Going all along, the Waitsburg, down that way. I've seen pictures of them, but I've never seen them work at all.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I wanted to ask you about the coming of the cars, the first... what you remember about the early day, the first car coming in the country. The first one that I remember anything about, when our neighbors had a car, Franklin. I said that about 1912, I imagine.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: They didn't have any top on it. It was on the other side, from what happened now, you know. On the right -hand side, and to say the left. And all the gears on that side, right where the door would be on that running board. All that running board then, too. The gears, the brake, and the honker. So he's down the road, you know, honked.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I guess about that time, I got a few doctors around town, got cars. But then people in the country couldn't use them. People in town couldn't get out in the country either in the wintertime, because no one mud and thing, you know. And they didn't clear the road, and they wouldn't have any road service or anything that time. We could get a car for about $1 ,000. We got our first car in 1917, I guess, to the Maxwell. The tires were so expensive and so hard to keep from cracking and deteriorating. And on the Sunday, the rubber'd crack, you know, and then they had the cotton fabric, and that would, that rubber cracked with the water in there, and rut that nut and it'd blow out, and I had a lot of tire trouble those days. If you had a tire trouble, you had to fix your own, too. They had to remember instead of wheels, the tire was on a rim, and you'd un -motor from the wheel and take it off and try to get the tire off the rim then. Some job would do that. And then the hydraulic jack, you had to get under the car and put it under the axle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He's never a jack, you know, you've probably seen him, haven't you?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: They didn't have to go very far under a car in those days. They were higher up anyway, though, right now. The Model A, the Acromodal T, was the best kind of car because they were high off the ground, didn't get hung up in deep ruts and keeps snowing, if I can tell you the thing. They were in the mountains where there were rocks and stumps doing the roadways, where there used to be. The car had got more and more cars, and the road was a dusty that they couldn't see where they were going when they had to do something about the roads then, you see. One thing followed another, and that thing.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: About 1917, 18, I think, there was a lot of cars coming into the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did your folks think about the cars when they first came in?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well, they thought, I don't know why folks often used to hear a lot of talk about it. They never mount anything because they just wasn't practical to have, and they scared too many horses and cost them their runaways and everything. I don't know what he asked for. I don't know what he asked for. I don't know what he asked for. Hello.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I bring your coster up in there?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Sure. That's okay.

words: Is that word, is that word?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Brandon met the Fleaners, I often. Yeah. They never met when he came there to write it. Every day the kid was going to read what they had written during the day and had a great time reading it because it was interesting. I know that they said it. He called Anselm's father, my husband's father, but he wanted to verify some of the facts because he'd been in the, in the, oh, the Doc War down there in Northern California. It was about that, you know. So he helped out with that some.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: So that's where, that's where they helped him, was on the, Henry Fleaner was the one that did most of the helping. He did most of the writing in his life. He did the actual writing of it, to understand.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: They called him that. Dave Fleaner, that's my husband's father, that brother of Henry's.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: He'd been in that same part of the country, they wrote about what he had. He wanted to get some, verify some of the facts about their writing. That part of the country only.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he read that book, haven't you?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: What I've read is, is a book by a man named Bate, about that book called Frontier Legend, I think. It was a book to show that that book was a bunch of lies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know that book?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I've read it, but I never read it, but I've already read it. It's such a book.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Well,

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: now, you're going to read it. I don't know. I don't know. I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think he lived very long after that, did he?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I think he died in 1913 or something like that in the southwest. I don't see how long he died. I don't see how long he died. He was in Seattle for a while, but I, I don't know. The story that Bate tells is that he died in the southwest. Well, it might have been some of that story that was not made up, but I know some of it wasn't either. I'm sure it wasn't all a fixing. But he was certainly working on the book when he was here in Moscow. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That's a story about him one way or another. I don't know. Well, I know is what I know. I mean, what you knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, when, when Dave's half brother was helping him, was he writing this, was he writing the stuff about Kit Carson and that sort of thing?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I guess I'll just go over it. Yes, and he wrote it down. He told, told him, told him that he couldn't even write. I don't know about that, but he wasn't able to put down what he wanted to say in order to write. It's been sad that he, he went to university and got it written. But I don't know why that, the truth in that, he might have rewritten or something. I don't know. I ended up there or something. I know that the Henry's kids, they were just younger, they liked to read his books. They'd written during the day, every day, you know. Because it's interesting. They might have written the whole thing, I think, for me and him. And it was told to him, to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, did they think that was true?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I suppose they did, I don't know. I've heard them, and they...

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Because Bates's story is that it wasn't true. I know. I've heard that, and they never tell me something about it. Not about that much.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The East High Dance is up there. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah. It's got a place where it's isolated, one thing, and it's hard to get to it, you know, up there in that mute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did people get up there?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The horseback, mostly, I guess.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Must have been some kind of road up there, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted a, I wanted a feat to get that road cut out, like it is now, I suppose you've been up there, haven't you?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I've never been up to the top of Bea, now. No, we just see it. My winding road, and it didn't ride wide either. And the scary look down over there, and I don't like that, I was born in a prairie country, I guess. I don't like these roads hung up on the side of the hills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember about it burning then?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Remember the inside of the university, out in the country where we lived, on an old one, it was sort of high. You see it burning in the middle of the night. We're looking out of our window, upstairs window, singing. That's all I remember about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many people mourned about the fact that it burned up to many of their records and their trophies and everything?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that kind of car that you were describing to me, was it a mud slinger?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The first kind of hot rods that I used to have. Well, the boys near the always, they were mechanical minded, they would see if they couldn't find the old motor and it picks up. And the chassis, you know, and they had a body to put it on, and they'd build a wooden seat and bed for it. Kind of a pickup thing out of it. More than a cost, for a seat in the back. No fenders, so they would see it. One of the fenders would sling the mud if they went through mud.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And we'd call them mud slingers.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It wasn't any better. It gave me any better with this. It gave me any better with this. I don't know, maybe a little better, but it doesn't sound much different to me.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I think I wouldn't have to talk about any of that anyway, but... You come if you're loud and clear on the tape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do much work out?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Yeah, I did a lot of work.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I said she was older than I am, and she wasn't very well, so she taught school, but she didn't work out. She had an education that she could teach. Those days she'd teach us and pass the examination. She taught school when she was 18. And she stayed home some summers, and I worked out in a different place around the neighborhood. I was good at sewing. I like to sew and sew. I had to be sewing and doing housework, more or less. Five dollars a week. I got paid. It was a pretty good pay. Six days.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Bought a piano, and I had an awful time paying for it, while I never really get entirely paid for it. I paid $100 for it. My dad paid for it. He didn't think I needed it. My mother spoke up for me, and so I got it. I worked there with people, and the piano, they wanted to get rid of it, and so I worked for them. I took the job before I knew anything about the piano, really, but I found out I wanted to read the piano, and I wanted the piano so bad that I got a chance to have it that way, between my wages over to my dad and all that I made for the different years that I worked out. I worked out for one family where they were bailing bailers. I don't know how many men there were at the table for, and they had so many flies. I went to work at the bedbugs, ate me up at night. I took the place to work.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I worked for this other people that had the piano. I worked there, and I worked there, cooking for the helping cook for the warehouse men. I had the sacks that came in, the sack paula grains at that time, and they had the pile of sacks in the warehouse at Fallon.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Whitman County, in Pullen, Clues. These people had had all the bedbugs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What work were you doing for them?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I was helping the main cook. They had a mailer crew or two, I don't know whether one or two crew, but I know a lot of men, bailing hay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stick with the job?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: Week, that's all I needed.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I didn't tell them about the bedbugs because I didn't want to complain, I guess, or something. I guess I was supposed to, if I did, I'd be in another room, but it was just bad. I probably would have, I told her when I settled up, but I thought I deserved more than five dollars a week. One dollar a day, I guess I was supposed to get a dollar a day there because I was being alone. I know the first night I went to bed, to the laptop, staring at the clothes, and didn't have like, just anything, of course. I was dead tired and got to bed off of late because I worked out in the dark, and I had to feed them after that, and they'd have to work afterwards. I was tired and I went to sleep right away, so I was commencing up, being something cold, inviting me, and I had an idea of what it was I'd never experienced before, but I'd heard about them. So I got up and looked, and I thought, well, I'd like to land, and they wouldn't bother me then because I'd been back there in the daytime, probably at night. I'd heard everyone else hearing out, and they don't, they buy them mostly. And I didn't stop them. So I let them have the bed, and I'd get down on the floor and lay on the rug. They beat me to the rug, I think. I couldn't land.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: When I settled up, I told her I didn't like the bed bugs, and I got up and out, and I'd have more pain because I worked so hard, and put them in somebody's seat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, why didn't you tell me I'd got to give you another room?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: But I still figured that the other thing would be just as bad as that.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: And I was a guy who never complained, and I took things that I can't believe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get more pain?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: I got ten dollars, I got all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you feel about the piano after you got it? Did you play piano before then?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: We hadn't argued before, and I loved it so much. But then I didn't have any other keyboard that I liked. I wanted more keys. I had no organ. I had so many octaves, and I had more. I got an awful good out of it, so I got married before I got paid for. I might have never paid the rest of it. I lacked thirty -five dollars to pay a hundred, I think. I worked for a dressmaker too in Pullman for quite a long time and worked for my board while I was working there, so I wouldn't have paid for my board. And I don't know what I got there, but I got more pay there, and I didn't, I worked for Dr. Rounds in Pullman too. It was a name work. The evening was so far away, I couldn't, I couldn't wait for my board and go to work downtown too. So I got another place with a preacher, his wife, met this preacher. That was a terrible place. Personally, it was Dr. Shan Jack, I love a place, and I didn't like him either. So I quit their place, and I don't know whether he left the country or why family left. Well, you know, they got transferred or whether I just quit, but I got another job of staying on my board, another couple. I had lots of fun with. They wanted me to pay five hundred, because I needed an extra hand, you know, to play cards. And I used to entertain them by kicking up, getting my bunny suit on at night, and trying to kick a light up in the ceiling. And they had this light on the thing, the thing from the ceiling, you know, drop a cord, I guess they called it, you know, and I didn't have any shade on or anything. But I had to do the washing there, and oh my, I had to do it in the basement, and I had to coat everything with coal, burn coal in the stove, you know, and the coal was smoking. They had to miss in there from the washing, couldn't see anything without a drop of ice, couldn't probably have the washing clean or anything. I didn't stay very long there either. Mrs. Gates, that was the person I was working for to do the dressmaking. She got ailing and couldn't keep up with the work, so she couldn't. I was going to go on the dressmaking by myself, and I had plenty of work that people would come and give me their work, but I just was tired out. I just couldn't sleep nights for worrying about the work, and I quit that and got married. So, in that I tried to go into school, but before I worked, before I started working in these places, and after I worked at Cassidy, that was a place I found at WSC at that time, at WSU now. One semester I thought I'd take some credits and learn how to teach, but I got sick of that because I couldn't get the subjects I wanted to, and it was as much fun as I thought it was going to be, so I quit that and got married. I got the 50 -footer job rather. The job, the president then went and got married, and after that, 1969, I was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's a bunny suit?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: A wall over a night -cold, you know, a beaten wall.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: That's what I thought.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know your husband for long before you got married?

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: It wasn't quite a long time because he was in the same neighborhood with us.

speaker: Dora Otter Fleener
words: The bachelor, I don't think he'd ever gotten married if I hadn't picked him up. There was something clear about it. There's a finger that cost the planes, you know, on 52 that you get about here. My husband's father was the only male left from that family. See, one boy died cross the plane, the other one died on TB. Left only Dave, and that family was the male. Well, he only had one son, which is Anselt, and my husband. And although that this Samuel that cost the plane of 52, he married again. He had four sons at that time, and none of those sons ever had a man child that carried on the name. So I'm the sole person that propagated the cleaner name. See, from that strength, I'd be a bit about very natural.
